activities_projects.title: The Circumcircle Challenge
activities_projects.objective: Construct a circle that passes through all three vertices of a triangle
steps: Construct a scalene triangle ABC with sides 6 cm, 7 cm, and 8 cm
Construct the perpendicular bisectors of at least two sides
Identify the point of intersection (Circumcenter)
Using the circumcenter as the center and the distance to any vertex as the radius, draw the circle
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Applies perpendicular bisector properties to find the circumcenter
activities_projects.evaluation: Circle passes perfectly through all three vertices (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Constructing the 75° and 105° Angles
activities_projects.objective: Use only a compass and straightedge to construct complex angles
steps: Construct a 90° angle and a 60° angle from the same vertex
Bisect the 30° gap between them to find the 75° line
Apply the same logic between 90° and 120° to find the 105° angle
Verify the accuracy with a protractor only after finishing
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Masters angle addition and bisection logic in construction
activities_projects.evaluation: Accuracy of the constructed angles within 1 degree (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Ambiguous SSA Case
activities_projects.objective: Investigate why Side-Side-Angle is not a sufficient congruence/construction condition
steps: Draw a line segment AB = 5 cm and an angle of 30° at A
Set the compass to $3$ cm and draw an arc from B
Observe the two different points where the arc intersects the $30^{\circ}$ ray
Construct both possible triangles and explain why the data was 'ambiguous'
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Analyzes the limitations of geometric data in unique constructions
activities_projects.evaluation: Successful construction of two distinct triangles from the same data (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Escher-Style Tiling Project
activities_projects.objective: Create a complex tiling by modifying a basic shape
steps: Cut out a 5x5 cm square from cardstock
Cut an irregular shape out of the left side and tape it to the right side
Repeat by cutting from the top and taping to the bottom
Trace this new 'jigsaw' shape repeatedly to tile an entire page
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands translation and rotation in advanced tilings
activities_projects.evaluation: Total coverage of the sheet with no gaps or overlaps (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: The Median Intersection (Centroid)
activities_projects.objective: Construct and verify the properties of medians
steps: Construct a triangle with sides 5 cm, 12 cm, and 13 cm (Right-angled)
Locate the midpoints of all three sides using perpendicular bisectors
Join each vertex to the opposite midpoint to draw the medians
Verify that all three medians meet at one point (Centroid)
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Masters the construction of medians and understands concurrency
activities_projects.evaluation: Precision of the centroid intersection (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Tiling with Regular Pentagons?
activities_projects.objective: Mathematically prove why certain regular polygons cannot tile
steps: Calculate the interior angle of a regular pentagon using $(n-2) 	imes 180 / n$
Try to fit three or four pentagons around a single point
Calculate the sum of the angles at the vertex (108° × 3)
Explain why the gap ($360 - 324$) prevents tiling
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Connects interior angle formulas to the possibility of tessellation
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct mathematical proof for the gap (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Altitudes and the Orthocenter
activities_projects.objective: Construct altitudes for an obtuse-angled triangle
steps: Draw an obtuse triangle (e.g., 110° angle)
Construct the altitudes from each vertex to the opposite side
Notice that you must extend the sides outward to find the meeting point
Identify the Orthocenter outside the triangle
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Handles the complexity of external altitudes and orthocenters
activities_projects.evaluation: Correct extension of sides and altitude accuracy (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Semi-Regular Archimedean Tiling
activities_projects.objective: Construct a tiling using Octagons and Squares
steps: Construct a regular octagon where each interior angle is 135°
Place squares (90°) in the gaps between octagons
Calculate the total angle at each vertex (135 + 135 + 90 = 360)
Create a colorful floor-pattern design using this rule
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Analyzes the geometry of dual-shape tilings
activities_projects.evaluation: Mathematical verification of the vertex sum (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Copying a Triangle (Construction Proof)
activities_projects.objective: Construct a congruent copy without measuring lengths
steps: Draw a random triangle ABC
On a separate line, copy side AB using only a compass
Copy $\angle A$ and $\angle B$ using the 'angle copying' construction method
Verify the new triangle is congruent using tracing paper
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Masters pure Euclidean construction without numerical measurement
activities_projects.evaluation: Exact congruence verified by superimposition (10 marks)

activities_projects.title: Tilings on a Grid (Isometric vs. Square)
activities_projects.objective: Compare tilings on different grid systems
steps: On square grid, attempt to tile a hexagon (observe the gaps)
On isometric grid, tile the same hexagon shape
Explain how the grid structure influences which shapes can tile perfectly
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes the relationship between grid geometry and tessellation
activities_projects.evaluation: Insightful comparison of the two grid types (10 marks)
